narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who went on to take the bar exam, looking at factors like grades, test scores, and background to forecast first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 36 (slightly under the 36.70 average), Year 3 school decile ranking of 1 (far below the average 5.53), Year 1 decile of 2 (versus an average 5.71), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 77% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The most influential factor pushing the model toward this failure prediction was your low Year 3 decile ranking, which signals weaker performance relative to peers in your later undergraduate years and strongly raised concerns about bar exam readiness. Next in importance, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing since it was close to average, showing solid aptitude in that area. Your middle family income bracket had a moderate influence toward the failure prediction, as the model associates it with slightly higher risk compared to higher brackets in the dataset. Overall, your academic deciles stood out as much lower than typical successful students, outweighing strengths like your near-average LSAT and common full-time status. While your background as a Black male brings unique strengths and perspectives to law, the model focused heavily on these performance metrics, which didn't align with profiles of those who typically pass the bar first time. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven flag on patterns seen in past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening your later-year grades through post-bac courses or a master's program, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience and non-academic achievements. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds significantly, and exploring schools with holistic reviews or bar passage support programs tailored to diverse students like yourself might open new doors. Keep pushing—you've already shown determination by applying.
